title: The Right Time to Raise Capital for an Australian SME Is Not When You Need It
linkedin:
The worst time to approach a lender is when you actually need the capital.

Yet for most Australian owner-led businesses, that is exactly when the conversation starts. Revenue is growing, an opportunity arrives, and the business seeks funding from a position of urgency rather than preparation.

The businesses that raise capital on the terms their financial profile warrants start three to six months before they need the funds. The window that creates the best conditions opens every July.

After EOFY, two things align that do not align at any other point in the year. Fresh accounts give lenders and investors a complete twelve months of trading data. And there is no urgency yet. The business has genuine runway to prepare, build the documentation, and control the narrative before any approach is made.

A lender reviewing July accounts sees a full year of trading rather than partial-period management accounts. An investor can build their own view of the business's trajectory without filling gaps. Both arrive at the conversation with a clearer picture, and the business arrives positioned rather than reactive.

The difference between a raise that progresses and one that stalls is almost always the quality of documentation. An information memorandum built properly before the first conversation is an evidence pack. It reconciles to the accounts, shows forward assumptions clearly, and gives a lender confidence about the people deploying the capital.

For Queensland and NSW businesses with a raise on the horizon in the next twelve months, the July accounts are the foundation to build from.

Full piece on the ProfitPulse blog.

One thing the most prepared capital raisers have in common: they started the conversation months before they needed the funds.

The post-EOFY window, July through September, gives Australian SMEs fresh accounts, no urgency, and time to build documentation that actually holds up under scrutiny. That combination is rare. Worth using deliberately.

google:
If a capital raise is on your horizon for the next twelve months, the weeks after June 30 are worth treating as a deliberate preparation window, not a quiet period.

This is the time of year when Australian SMEs are best positioned to begin a capital raise. EOFY accounts give lenders and investors a complete twelve-month view of the business. There is no urgency driving the conversation yet. Together, those two conditions mean you can approach any lender or investor from a position of preparation rather than pressure.

The businesses that get the best outcome from a capital raise tend to have one thing in common: they started the documentation work before anyone asked for it. An information memorandum that reconciles to the accounts, presents forward assumptions clearly, and explains the use of funds in plain terms creates a very different conversation than one assembled under deal pressure.

Common issues that create friction in a capital conversation include an owner salary set for tax efficiency rather than at a market rate, one-off costs without explanation, and working capital projections that do not show their assumptions. Each of these is manageable when there is time to address it. Each becomes an obstacle when the conversation has already started.

For Queensland, NSW, and Victorian business owners with a raise planned in the next financial year, July is the natural starting point.

Read the full post for a practical breakdown of what to prepare and why the timing matters: https://profit-pulse.com.au/the-right-time-to-raise-capital-for-an-australian-sme-is-not-when-you-need-it
